sections.heading: What is an Activity?
sections.content: An Activity represents a single screen in an Android app, such as a login page or home screen. It handles user interaction and displays the UI. Activities are managed by the Android system and must be declared in the AndroidManifest.xml.

sections.heading: Key Characteristics of an Activity
items: Represents one screen with a user interface
Has a lifecycle managed by Android OS
Can communicate with other Activities using Intents

sections.heading: What is the Activity Lifecycle?
sections.content: The Activity lifecycle is the sequence of states an Activity goes through from creation to destruction. Android provides lifecycle callback methods so the app can manage resources and respond to state changes properly.

rows.purpose: Activity is destroyed and resources are released

sections.heading: Lifecycle Flow
items: User returns to app → onRestart → onStart → onResume
User presses Back → onPause → onStop → onDestroy

sections.heading: Activity Launch Modes
items: standard – default behavior, new instance every time
singleTop – reuse if already at top of stack
singleTask – single instance in the task
singleInstance – runs in its own task

sections.heading: Best Practices
items: Release resources in onPause() or onStop()
Use ViewModel to survive configuration changes
Avoid heavy work inside lifecycle callbacks

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Activities are the foundation of Android UI. Understanding lifecycle callbacks helps developers manage resources correctly, handle configuration changes, and create stable Android applications.
